db_statements: (A. L. M. Nicolas’ “Siyyid ‘Alí-Muḥammad dit le Báb,” p. 290.)
“M. Mochenin says in his memoirs concerning the Báb: ‘In the month of June, 1850 … having gone to C̱hihríq on duty, I saw the Bálá-Ḵhánih from the heights of which the Báb taught his doctrine.’”
“Dr. Cormick was an English physician long resident in Tabríz, where he was highly respected.”
He gives his personal impressions of the Báb, having been sent with two other Persian doctors to examine whether the Báb was sane.
Dr. Cormick’s account of his impressions of the Báb was extracted from “letters written by him to the Rev. Benjamin Labaree, D.D.”
“The document was communicated to Professor E. G. Browne … by Mr. W. A. Shedd,” son of the late Rev. J. H. Shedd of the American Mission at Urúmíyyih.
“the late Rev. J. H. Shedd, D.D., of the American Mission at Urúmíyyih, Persia, of the same mission as Dr. Benjamin Labaree”
“It was at Marand,” writes Lady Sheil, “that I first heard the aḏhán, or call of the Muslims to prayer.” (“Glimpses of Life and Manners in Persia.”)
“The late Ḥájí Mírzá Jání writes: ‘I myself met him [Mírzá Muḥammad-Ḥasan, the younger brother of Mullá Ḥusayn] when he was bringing his mother and sister from Karbilá to Qazvín.’”
“It was then, says Mírzá Jání, that Islám gave a shameful exhibition to the world.”
“Mírzá Muḥammad-Ḥasan, the younger brother of Mullá Ḥusayn”
“After the death of Jináb-i-Bábu’l-Báb, Ḥaḍrat-i-Quddús bestowed on him the sword and turban of that glorious martyr, and made him captain of the troops of the True King.”
“His sister was the wife of S̱hayḵh Abú-Turáb of Qazvín, who was a scholar and philosopher such as is rarely met with and believed with the utmost sincerity and purity of purpose.”
“The sister of Mullá Ḥusayn was surnamed ‘Varaqatu’l-Firdaws’ and was intimately associated, while in Karbilá, with Ṭáhirih.” (“Memorials of the Faithful,” p. 270.)

canonical_name: Riḍá Khán, son of Muḥammad Khán the Turkamán
db_statements: “Riḍá Ḵhán, the son of Muḥammad Ḵhán the Turkamán, Master of the Horse to his late Majesty Muḥammad S̱háh … a youth graceful of form, comely of face, endowed with all manner of talents and virtues.”
“the prince, because of the exceeding hatred he bore Riḍá Ḵhán ordered him to be hewn in pieces.”
“Muḥammad Ḵhán the Turkamán, Master of the Horse to his late Majesty Muḥammad S̱háh”
“in company with Mírzá Sulaymán-Qulí of Núr (a son of the late S̱háṭir-Bás̱hí, also conspicuous for his virtues, learning, and devotion), came to Ṭihrán.”
“The most notable of these was Mullá Sa‘íd of Zarkanád. Now he was a man so accomplished in science.”
“they sent him to Sárí in chains and fetters and there slew him under circumstances of the utmost cruelty along with his companions.”
“certain learned men of the kindred of Mullá Muḥammad-Taqí of Núr addressed to Jináb-i-Quddús in writing certain questions touching the science of divination and astrology.”
“S̱hayḵh Ṣáliḥ of S̱híráz went to extinguish the fire. A bullet struck his head and shattered his skull.”

canonical_name: Áqá Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad
db_statements: “a second bullet carried away the hand of Áqá Mírzá Muḥammad ‘Alí, the son of Siyyid Aḥmad who was the father of Áqá Siyyid Ḥusayn, ‘the beloved.’”
“Áqá Siyyid Ḥusayn ‘the beloved,’ a child ten years of age slain before his father’s eyes and he fell rolling in mud and gore, with limbs quivering like those of a half-killed bird.”

“The bullet struck Siyyid Riḍá full in the chest and killed him instantly. He was a man of pure and simple ways, of deep and sincere convictions. Out of respect for his master he always walked alongside of his horse.”
“it was Mírzá Luṭf-‘Alí, the secretary who drew his dagger and stabbed Ḵhusraw.”
“it was Mírzá Luṭf-‘Alí, the secretary who drew his dagger and stabbed Ḵhusraw.”
“thirty Bábís whose chief, Mírzá Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín, pupil of the late S̱hayḵh Aḥmad-i-Aḥsá’í, was an elderly, pious and respected gentleman.”
“referred the case to the governor of the province, Prince Ḵhanlan Mírzá. He, however, paid no attention to their grievances, having many other preoccupations.”
“sent Muḥammad Big, Yávar (captain), at the head of three hundred men, to restore order.”
“Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Ḵhán called for his brother ‘Abdu’lláh.”
“Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Ḵhán, brother of ‘Abdu’lláh, received substantial tokens of the royal favor.”

“‘Abbás-Qulí Ḵhán-i-Láríjání sent for Muḥammad-Sulṭán and ‘Alí Ḵhán of Savád-Kúh.”
“Mírzá Áqá, Mustawfí of Mázindarán, superintendent of finances, the head of the ‘Ulamás and many other men of high standing.”

canonical_name: Sulṭán-Ḥusayn Mírzá, son of Fatḥ-‘Alí Sháh
db_statements: “they were Sulṭán Ḥusayn Mírzá, son of Fatḥ-‘Alí S̱háh, and Dawúd Mírzá.” (Gobineau, killed in the Ṭabarsí fighting.)
“and Dawúd Mírzá, son of Ẓillu’s-Sulṭán, uncle of the S̱háh.”
“A. L. M. Nicolas, in his ‘Siyyid ‘Alí-Muḥammad dit le Báb’ (p. 308), adds Mustawfí Mírzá ‘Abdu’l-Báqí.” (slain among the royalist officers)
“he entrusted Ḥájí Ḵhán Núrí and Mírzá ‘Abdu’lláh Navayy with the responsibility of securing adequate supplies.”
“he entrusted Ḥájí Ḵhán Núrí and Mírzá ‘Abdu’lláh Navayy with the responsibility of securing adequate supplies.”
“then Naṣru’lláh Ḵhán-i-Bandibí, another chieftain.”
“and Muṣṭafá Ḵhán from As̱hraf to whom he gave the command of the brave tufang-chis of that city.”
“Thus departed in great haste Muḥsin Ḵhán-i-As̱hrafí with his cavalry, a troop of Afg̱háns.”
“Muḥammad-Karím Ḵhán-i-As̱hrafí with some of the tufang-chis of the town.”
“and Ḵhalíl Ḵhán of Savád-Kúh with the men of Qádí-Kalá.”
“the command was taken from Mihdí-Qulí Mírzá and given to the Afs̱hár Sulaymán Ḵhán, a man of acknowledged firmness and of great influence.”
“Sulaymán Ḵhán-i-Afs̱hár, recently sent by the S̱háh, was urging such a method.”
“the other Mírzá Muḥammad-Báqir-i-Ḵhurásáníy-i-Qá’iní, cousin of our Bábí author … it was he who designed the fortifications of S̱hayḵh Ṭabarsí.”
“Qá’iní, breaking his bonds, jumped upon him, snatched his sword and struck him with such violence that his head rolled about fifteen feet away.”
